observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Municipal Accountant:
Prepare and submit to the audit team the BRS including the supporting documents of all bank accounts of the Municipality within ten (10) days after the receipt of the bank statements from the servicing banks;
Submit the BRS for CY 2023 and the BRS from January to March 2024 until May 15, 2024; and
Review the bank account in the PNB and the purpose of the creation of the account together with the Municipal Treasurer, and if warranted, close the account and the remaining balance shall be deposited to the general fund account of the Municipality.  Effect the necessary journal entries to record any deposit made.

We recommended that the Management:
Require the Municipal Accountant, as part of her pre-audit function, and the Municipal Mayor, being the approving officer, to disapprove the grant of additional cash advances in excess of the maximum cash accountability of each accountable officer set by rules and regulations on the grant and utilization of cash advances.  If the increase in accountability is in exigency of the LGU’s mandate, the prescribed rate of bond must be strictly observed pursuant to Treasury Circular No. 02-2009 dated August 6, 2009; and
Require the Accountable Officers to strictly observe the prescribed period of liquidations of cash advances to avoid the accumulation of cash accountabilities.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal Treasurer to immediately deposit/refund/liquidate his accounted cash shortage totaling P145,414.73.  Failure to do so will result in the elevation of the matter to the COA Regional Director thru our Legal Services Division for evaluation and referral to the Office of the Ombudsman, if warranted, for proper filing of appropriate charges for the acts committed in violation of Section 105(2) of PD No. 1445, and be held liable for the missing/misappropriated government funds in accordance with Section 340 of RA No. 7160.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant:
Review the records of the Due from Other Funds and Due to Other Funds accounts in all funds and post the necessary adjustments to present the correct balances of the reciprocal accounts;
Make a reconciliation of the reciprocal accounts every year-end to present an adjusted and reconciled balances in the Financial Statements; and
Stop using the SEF and TF for general fund expenditures and immediately facilitate the transfer of the amount due to SEF and TF so that these will be readily available for the intended purpose of the fund, pursuant to Section 272 of the Republic Act No. 7160 and P.D. No. 1445.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant:
Review the entries made in the subsidiary ledgers of the aforementioned accounts to identify the errors made and post the necessary adjustments to present the correct balances of the accounts; and
Ensure that every year-end report, balances of these accounts are correct for fair and accurate presentation in the Financial Statements.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant:
Make an appointment with the GSIS Bayombong Office to identify the list of employees in their records that have unpaid premiums or over remittance, and reconcile the same with the records of the LGU, including the remittances made that were not recognized by the GSIS BO, so that full benefits will be enjoyed by the employees;
Make sure that the government and personal shares are properly withheld and remitted; and
Update regularly the GSIS BO as to the changes of status, positions, salaries, and other pertinent information of every employee to avoid any issues in the future.

We recommended that the Management:
Strictly abide by the procedures and guidelines set forth in COA-DBM-DILG-GCG-DND Joint Circular No. 2015-01 dated January 8, 2015 to fully strengthen peace and order in the community so that the purpose of the appropriated confidential funds will be served; and
See to it that the appropriated amounts will be utilized in accordance with rules, laws and regulations, and not become idle so that the benefits shall be felt and enjoyed by the community.

We recommended that the Management:
Instruct the Municipal Treasurer and concerned offices or personnel to re-examine and re-evaluate the collection efforts made and identify the issues or circumstances that constrained the realization of the targeted collections, especially the Tax Revenue-Goods and Services.    For the next budget preparation, consider the information gathered to have more reasonable projection of collections and to improve the collection efforts of the Municipality, so that these will not negatively impact the funding of the prioritized PPAs of the Municipality; and
Instruct the Municipal Budget Officer to prepare and submit the complete SAAOB for current and continuing appropriation for CY 2023 of all the funds of the Municipality on or before May 30, 2024.  And for the succeeding SAAOB every year, submit the complete set to the Accounting Office as a support in the Financial Statements of the Municipality which will eventually be submitted to the COA on or before February 14 of each year.

We recommended the following:
Accounting Office and the Municipal DRRM Office continue their efforts to work hand in hand to properly account and maintain appropriate records and reports on the transfer and reversion of LDDRM funds to expedite fiscal management, report generation and improve the transparency of the utilization of the funds; and
Accounting Office to create and maintain separate subsidiary ledgers for each of the unutilized STF every yearend and to record any expenditures related thereof.   With this measure, management and monitoring of the funds and generating reports will be more efficient and effective, and renders a more reliable balance reflected in the financial statements.

We recommended that the:
Local Chief Executive, being the primary responsible official of the Municipality, require the Municipal Accountant, Municipal Treasurer, and all other concerned personnel to submit immediately the unsubmitted financial reports, transaction documents (vouchers and official receipts) for post-audit.  Otherwise, non-submission may result to audit suspension or disallowance;
Require the concerned offices and personnel the strict observance of the prescribed submission dates of mandatory reports as required under Section 100 of PD 1445 and Section 7.2.1 (a) of COA Circular No. 2009-006; and
Require the concerned personnel in the Accounting Office to ensure the performance of their main official function and responsibilities by producing an organized, complete, and accurate financial reports as an end-product of the accounting system, and submit thereof to the Office of the Auditor within the prescribed period.

We recommended that the Management comply with the regulations and furnish the Office of the Auditor the required copies of purchase orders and contracts together with complete supporting documents within five (5) working days from the execution of a contract or issuance of PO for the timely review, evaluation, and inspection of the same, in adherence to COA Circular 2009-001.
We recommended also that the Accounting personnel ensure that the transmittal of the attached contracts or POs were duly stamped “received” by COA and attached to the vouchers before the payments are processed and disbursed, pursuant to COA Circular No. 2023-004, otherwise, payments may be suspended in audit.

We recommended that the Management require the BAC and the GSO to post the RFQs of the procurements through SVP with above P50,000.00 ABCs in the PhilGEPS, in the agency’s website, and at any conspicuous place reserved for such purpose in the premises of the Municipality, in adherence with of Item 8 (b) of Annex H of the RIRR of RA 9184.

We recommended that the Management strictly implement the posting of warranty security on the procurement of goods and formulate a policy or guidelines in the application thereof, in accordance with Section 62.1 of the Revised IRR of RA No. 9184.

Submit an explanation on allowing the Supplier as eligible even with the above-mentioned lapses;
Require the BAC and the BAC TWG to conduct a more vigilant post-qualification to ensure that the winning bidders are legally, technically and financially capable to deliver or supply the items being procured to avoid unnecessary risks and putting the Municipality in a disadvantageous position.

We recommended that the Management:
Submit a justification and the analysis made of the three (3) banks’ offers that would explain why the Eastwest bank’s offer was accepted, even if the Landbank and DBP had better proposals that are more advantageous to the government;
Explain also why the avoidable loan interest of P6,918,493.15 or one percent (1%) per annum of the loan should not be disallowed in audit when payment thereof is made; and
Always bear in mind that management of government funds and properties in performing its mandate should be in line with efficiency, economy, and effectiveness.

observations.recommendation: 1.6	We recommended the Municipal Accountant to prepare an adjusting journal entry to take up the unutilized balance of the BGCM by debiting Prior Year Adjustments and crediting the Due to NGAS account. The resulting report should be submitted to the Office of the Auditor within the period prescribed under COA Circular 2020-009;

Inform the Accounting Office and the GSO of donations in-kind received by the LGU to facilitate the monitoring of goods and its recognition in the books of the LGU for CY 2021.

3.6	We recommended that management explain/justify their purchase of the COVID-19 testing kits from suppliers without FDA authorizations to avoid the suspension of the transaction. Moreover, we recommended that management directly procure COVID-19 testing kits to suppliers who are authorized by the FDA to ensure the provision of accurate and reliable information regarding results of COVID-19 tests.

4.7	We recommended the Accounting Office to rectify the accounts used in recording the transaction through a correcting entry. Necessary adjustment should be made to record the effect of liquidation documents submitted thereafter. We further recommended the Management to advise the concerned Implementing Agency on the submission of utilization report / liquidation documents for proper validation, verification of the Audit Team, and for appropriate adjustment by the Accounting Office.

Require the requisitioning office to always be specific in describing the items they are about to purchase and to carefully assess the quantity of items based on their needs;
Submit the RDS of the missing 6,458 pieces of washable face masks; and
Adhere to the guidelines and updates set by the DOH and other government agencies in battling COVID-19.

6.9	We recommended that management submit the lacking documentary requirements on the procurement of the sanitation tents to establish the legality and capability of the supplier and to update the GSO and Accounting records as to who acknowledged the unaccounted nine (9) units of open tents. Failure to immediately submit the lacking requirements shall lead to the issuance of Notice of Suspension (NS) and subsequent failure to settle this NS shall further lead to the disallowance of the transaction for being irregular in audit.

8.8	We recommended that the management update the three (3) FDP Boards of the LGU and constantly and timely submit the required documents in the FDP Portal pending the establishment of the LGU’s internet domain. Moreover, comply on the posting of the monthly Report on Fund Utilization and Status of Implementation of BGCM PPAs in at least three (3) conspicuous public places in the locality.

9.6	We recommended that the Management require the Liquidating Officer to fully deposit all her collections to the depository bank and install a system of internal control in the Municipal Treasury Office that will ensure that issuance of official receipts is supported by actual receipt of collections.

10.9	We recommended management to require Mr. Gaudencio J. dela Cruz to immediately deposit/refund/liquidation his cash shortage totaling P3,038,010.29. Failure of the immediate refund/deposit/liquidation of his accountability will result to the elevation of the matter to the Office of the Ombudsman, if warranted, for the proper filing of the appropriate charges for the acts committed in violation of Section 105(2) of P.D. 1445, and be held liable for the missing/misappropriated government funds in accordance with Section 340 of R.A. 7160.

11.10	We recommended management to advise the Disbursing Officer to use the standard format of a Liquidation Report in liquidating his cash advances and to liquidate them within the legal deadline in accordance with the applicable rules and regulations before another cash advance could be granted to him. Any unexpended balance of the cash advance should be refunded and be issued with an Official Receipt as part of his supporting documents in the liquidation.
11.11	We also recommended management to stop the withdrawals for cash payments to suppliers, obligations, travels and other expenses.  Payments should be made thru checks as cited in the regulations, except for those petty expenses which may be paid thru petty cash fund.  All existing withdrawals should be liquidated and closed. Excess funds must be returned to each appropriate fund properly.
11.12	Payments for payrolls, wages and petty expenses should be withdrawn under the regular cash advances and special cash advances granted must be payments for the specified purpose only.
11.13	Strict compliance with the existing rules and regulations on cash advances is enjoined.

12.5	We recommended the increase of the fidelity bond of the Disbursing Officer who is also designated as Trust Fund Liquidating Officer in accordance with the Bureau of Treasury Circular No. 2-99 dated August 6, 2009 since his average total cash handled for the latest six (6) months of P9,578,527.00 is way beyond his approved maximum accountability of only P750,000.00.
12.6	We also recommended management to designate other personnel of the Treasury Office to fully handle the collections on the Trust Fund in order to reduce the Disbursing Officer’s cash accountability and in order for him to focus more on his function.

14.6	We recommended that Management take action on the fourteen (14) prior-year projects that were still recorded under Construction-in-Progress account by updating their individual statuses and pursue their full implementation.  Moreover, reclassify and recognize the Depreciation Expense for the year and the previous years’ if any, for completed projects still lodged to the CIP account. 15.5	We recommended that the Accountant reconcile the affected accounts, effect the necessary adjustments and cause the liquidations or return of borrowed funds. Attention should be strictly focused especially on the Trust Fund Cash account which should be kept intact because the outlay from these funds shall only be for their intended purpose and not to be subjected to temporary borrowings.

16.5	We recommended Management to trace the custody and history of records of the accounts Advances to Contractors, Prepaid Expenses and the Biological Assets of the LGU for the proper adjustment of the accounts, preparation of supporting schedules and determination of proper accountability for government funds and property.

17.11	We recommended that the management set a fund for the payment of the unsettled past due insurance premium of three heavy equipment with the GSIS as a requisite for its renewal in 2021 and to avoid the consequences of being at default with the agreement entered with the DBP.

19.5	We recommended that Management enforce immediate settlement of the disallowances and comply with Section 7.1.1 of the Revised Rules and Regulations on Settlement of Accounts as implemented under COA Circular No. 2009-006. Moreover, we recommended that payments made by liable persons be presented to the Audit Team by the Municipal Treasurer in order to provide them with Notice of Settlements and subsequent removal of their disallowances in the COA record.

20.8	We recommended that Management timely and completely submit to the Auditor copies of monthly trial balances, quarterly financial statements, monthly journals and complete disbursement vouchers and report on collections and deposits, in compliance with COA Circular No. 2009-006 and Section 111 of PD 1445. Furthermore, we recommended that the LCE place internal controls (ex. routing slip) on the processing of financial transactions passing through certifying and authorizing officials as well as  monitor and expedite the Municipal Treasurer’s Office submission of collection and disbursement vouchers to the Accounting Office’ which shall likewise submit to COA after recording/journalizing without delay. Otherwise, the penalty being imposed for non-submission shall be strictly enforced.

22.11	We recommended that:
The BAC-TWG carefully and sufficiently examine, validate and verify the eligibility, technical capability and financial requirements of winning bidders necessary for post-qualification and to coordinate with the Engineering Office and the Accounting Office on the status of the previously awarded contracts to the winning bidder;
The Engineering Office conduct an inspection as at the target completion date of the contract in order to serve as basis in computing liquidated damages and negative slippages, and to adopt the calibrated actions in response to delays in the implementation of infrastructure projects as per the GPPB Circular stated above; and
The management require the Engineering Office an update on the construction of the two (2) projects with known negative slippages and to conduct an investigation on why did the AMP Construction abandon the construction of the school building in Cawayan Elementary School and settle the issue first before new contract could be awarded.

24.5	We recommended that Management require the requisitioning office to indicate the description/technical specifications of items to be procured in the Purchase Request, Abstract of Quotations and other documentary requirements in order to apprise the approving authorities and the suppliers of the items needed by the end user. Moreover, we recommended the Bids and Awards Committee to monitor all the purchase requests and notify the requisitioning office immediately for compliance of the lacking description/specification before processing the request.

25.19	We recommended that the management require the end user or the requisitioning office to fully describe or indicate the important details in the Technical Specifications of goods to be procured that is commensurate to the purpose to which those good are to be used. Moreover, submit an explanation as to why the management still pursued the transaction despite the bidder’s failure to deliver the operational truck that was subjected for the bidding.

observations.managementAction: 2019 AAR | Close coordination between the Management and the District heads were made for optimal utilization of the SEF.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that Management direct the Municipal Accountant to enforce that all payments for the procurement of goods and services be made by check directly to suppliers or dealers in order to withhold taxes due the government. As one of the withholding agents, the appropriate withholding taxes should be computed and reflected on the face of the Disbursement Voucher and the corresponding Certificate of Withholding Tax form be issued to concerned suppliers or creditors. Moreover, we recommended that personnel who further claim reimbursements despite of the observation shall bear the payment of the withheld tax. To remedy the situation, collect in cash the should-be amount of tax withheld from the suppliers by the issuance of an official receipt and remit the collected amounts to the BIR.
observations.managementAction: 2019 AAR | The applicable withholding tax were deducted to all reimbursements above P1,000.00, except for reimbursements of fuel expenses and in cases of emergency purchase.

observations.recommendation:
2.6	We recommended that:
The Municipal Treasurer requires all accountable officers/collectors the full remittance of their collections daily. This will discourage possible personal use or misappropriation of the fund at their hands;
The Treasury Office sets a cut-off time for the other collectors’ remittances to the LO (i.e. every 4:00 PM) and weekly deposit to the depository bank to ensure the full compliance with the regulations and to safeguard government funds; and
The Management considers the transfer of their depository accounts to LBP or DBP Branch that is nearest to the LGU’s respective areas of jurisdiction (Sec. 311, LGC) in order to facilitate the regular deposit of collections and to save attributable costs related in deposits/withdrawal such as per diems and gas.

3.6	We recommended that the Treasury Office:
Sustain a reliable logbook of accountable forms and religiously prepare monthly Report of Accountability for Accountable Forms, indicating therein the procured and unissued accountable forms; and
Issue a circular or notice of loss in cases of loss of accountable forms for the information and guidance of all concerned to prevent the possible fraudulent use of such accountable forms.

4.6	We recommended that:
The DO submit liquidation reports or issue an Official Receipt of P1,242,471.49 and deposit the said amount to settle his unliquidated cash advance, and liquidate his cash advances within its legal deadline in accordance with the applicable rules and regulations. Any unexpended balance of the cash advance should be refunded and be issued with an Official Receipt as part of his supporting documents in the liquidation; and
The Municipal Treasurer monitor regularly the cash advances and liquidations of the DO.

5.5	We recommended that the Management specify the purpose/s of the cash advances granted clearly showing the obligations to be paid and the amount thereof and supply the necessary documents to establish its propriety.

6.6	We recommended that the Accounting Office records only transactions that are complete as to documentary requirements, already paid and transmitted to them for recording, in order to avoid discrepancies between accounting and treasury records.

7.6	We recommended that Management:
Advise the Municipal Accountant and the Inventory Committee on Property, Plant and Equipment (PPE) to reconcile the above accounts and submit reports for validation;
Verify and book up any unrecorded PPE at historical cost together with its accumulated depreciation if records are available, if none, at appraised value or replacement cost; and
Submit Journal Entry Voucher (JEV) for the reconciliation/adjustments made thereat.

8.9	We recommended that the Management require the GSO to submit a Summary of Supplies and Materials Issued (SSMI) to the Accounting Office for their basis in dropping subject inventories from their books and to conduct a semestral physical inventory of unissued supplies and materials. We recommended further that the Accounting Office adjust the account to better reflect the amount in the financial statements and to recognize expendable and semi-expendable properties as expense once issued, based on COA Circular No. 2005-002.
9.5	We recommended that the Management trace the custody and history of records of the biological assets of the LGU for proper accountability and adjustment of accounts.

10.8	We recommended that the Management:
a. 	Require the GAD Focal Point System/Focal Person to ensure that the Municipality’s GPB is prepared and appropriately evaluated by the PPDO to ensure that PPAs to be undertaken are in line with the GAD priority agenda of the Provincial Government and that activities are sufficiently reviewed and endorsed by the DILG;
b. 	Educate each department with trainings and seminars regarding GAD planning and budgeting concepts to enhance and broaden their knowledge in planning and proper execution of GAD PPAs; and
c. 	Follow the timelines in the preparation of GAD Plan and Budget and GAD Accomplishment Reports.
11.4	We recommended that the Municipal Mayor with the help of the Sangguniang Bayan, formulate and adopt a local GAD Code.
12.5	We recommended that the Management monitor their GPB, determine the cause/s of its slow implementation and make corrective measures to expedite its execution.

13.8	We recommended that the Management observe the general limitations on personnel services cited under Section 325(a) of RA No. 7160. The Municipal Mayor, being the Local Chief Executive, always be guided by the conditions of the agency’s annual and supplemental budgets to ensure that the budgeting requirements were completely complied with. Furthermore, the Human Resource Management Officer, the Municipal Budget Officer and the Municipal Accountant propose possible solution for the elimination of excess cost of the PS to forestall audit disallowance as provided under Section 3.15.1 of COA Circular No 2012-003. We further recommended the reduction of the bonuses and allowances given to each officials and employees.
14.8	We recommended that the Management observe the 45% Personnel Services limitation of the LGU during budget preparations and initiate to return the amount disbursed for CREA.
15.11	We recommended that the Management submit a legal basis to warrant the giving of the said CREA to aid in the determination of the propriety and validity of the disbursements.

16.5	We recommended that the Accounting Department monitor the accumulation of the P90,000.00 limit for bonuses and other benefits of each government official and employee and consider the excess in computing compensation income of that individual.

18.5	We recommended that the Management enforce immediate settlement of the disallowances and comply with Section 7.1.1 of the Revised Rules and Regulations on Settlement of Accounts as implemented under COA Circular No. 2009-006. Moreover, we recommended that payments made by liable persons be presented to the Audit Team in order to provide them with Notice of Settlements and subsequent removal of their disallowances in the record.

19.8	We recommended that Management timely submit to the Auditor the hardcopies of monthly trial balances, quarterly financial statements, monthly journals and reports as well as complete disbursement vouchers and vouchers on collections and deposits, in compliance with COA Circular No. 2009-006 and Section 111 of PD 1445. Furthermore, request for the submission of important reports and documents shall be observed as directed by the same PD.

20.7	We recommended that the Municipality, through the Municipal Disaster Risk Reduction and Management Office, prudently evaluate its compliance with the Minimum Standards on Disaster Preparedness of the National Disaster Risk Reduction and Management Council and conform accordingly with its provisions to achieve desired goals and objectives of reducing disaster risk and enhancing disaster preparedness and response capabilities of the LGU.

21.5	We recommended that the LDRRMO submit the Report on the Sources and Utilizations of LDRRMF, duly prepared and certified correct by the Municipal Accountant, on or before the 15th day after the end of each month to the Office of the Auditor as prescribed under COA Circular No. 2012-002 dated September 12, 2012.
22.7	We recommended that the LDRRMO review and update their LDRRMFIP at the beginning of the year to include programs, projects and activities funded from the STF.

23.8	We recommended that the Management take into practice the submission of complete accounts in order to support reports that are relevant to audit and submit the lacking MDRRM accounts in order to validate the transferred amount.

24.6	We recommended that Management comply with the proper utilization of LDRRMF and guarantee that disbursements are in relation to disaster prevention, mitigation, preparedness, response, rehabilitation and recovery projects and activities. We further recommended that Management return the total amount of prohibited disbursements of P840,788.27 to the Special Trust Fund.
25.5	We recommended that the Accountant reconcile the affected accounts and effect the necessary adjustments.

26.7	We recommended that the management:
Install a good system of internal control or a process flow on the renting of heavy equipment to LGU clients from application to its eventual return;
Revise portions of the Local Revenue Code of the Municipality by updating heavy equipment items available for rent as well as its rates per hour; and
Shift the renting and monitoring of the heavy equipment to the GSO to install a good separation of duties from the Engineering Office.

27.7	We recommended that the Management and the LSB:
Identify programs which were yet to be implemented, or reprogram these if needed;
Review the total cash available which were un-programmed due to overshooting of estimated income; and
Ensure that all projects/programs/activities in the approved budget of SEF were the priority of the schools so that high rate of implementation, if not full, would be met.

28.5	We recommended that the Management and the LSB screen out locally-hired teachers and consider only those whose names appear in the RQA of the DepEd. Moreover, existing personnel under the SEF initiate their application and examination to the DepEd in order to qualify themselves in the shortlist of the RQA.

29.4	We recommended that the Management request the Sangguniang Bayan to issue a resolution authorizing the closure of the said bank account and the immediate transfer to the other bank accounts of the LGU.

30.6	We recommended that the Management:
Diligently monitor the monthly remittance of mandatory deductions and see to it that all these deductions are remitted in full; and
Avoid over-remittance at the expense of the LGU.

31.9	We recommended that:
The Management expedite the implementation of all pending and on-going funded programs under the 20% DF including backlog PPAs from the previous years in order to optimize the utilization thereof and timely bring the desired benefits to the intended recipients;
The Engineering Office submit quarterly inspection reports as to the stage of completion of all on-going projects in addition to the accomplishment reports they are quarterly submitting; and
The MPDC monitor the implementation of all the PPAs funded by the 20% DF.

32.7	We recommended that the Management return the unutilized balances of the PDAF and fully implemented projects sourced from NGAs to the Bureau of the Treasury amounting to P500,000.00 and to the Source Agencies totaling P540,536.79 as to comply with the final PDAF Decision of the Supreme Court and to help the national government in its fiscal administration.

33.8	We recommended that:
The Management return the ownership of the native animal modules to the LGU by providing the Accounting Office the necessary documents and to look into the whereabouts of the native chicken module and its eventual recognition;
The Accounting Office recognize the cost of the constructed housing unit to the financial statement; and
The LGU refrain from constructing or improving facilities to privately-owned land that would give rise to related-party transactions in violation of R.A. No. 6713.

We recommended that the Municipal Government prepare, submit and approve its annual budget based on a realistic and attainable income to achieve a balanced budget, that is, income is sufficient to cover all its expenditures. Furthermore, we recommended that the LFC formulate measures that will create and generate additional sources of income but must consider the generating capacity of the LGU in its determination of income reasonably projected as collectible for the ensuing year.

observations.recommendation: 2.7	We recommended that the Management require the GSO to submit a Summary of Supplies and Materials Issued (SSMI) to the Accounting Office for their basis in dropping subject inventories from their books and to conduct a semestral physical inventory of unissued supplies and materials. We recommended further that the Accounting Office adjust the account to better reflect the amount in the financial statements and to recognize semi-expendable properties as expense once issued.
3.5	We recommended that the Management trace the custody and history of records of the biological assets of the LGU for proper accountability and adjustment of accounts.

4.13	We recommended that the Municipal Government prepare, submit and approve its annual budget based on a realistic and attainable income to achieve a balanced budget, that is, income is sufficient to cover all its expenditures. Furthermore, we recommended that the LFC formulate measures that will create and generate additional sources of income but must consider the generating capacity of the LGU in its determination of income reasonably projected as collectible for the ensuing year.

5.7	We recommended that the Management observe the general limitations on personnel services cited under Section 325(a) of RA No. 7160. The Municipal Mayor, being the Local Chief Executive, always be guided by the conditions of the agency’s annual and supplemental budgets to ensure that the budgeting requirements were completely complied with. Furthermore, the Human Resource Management Officer, the Municipal Budget Officer and the Municipal Accountant propose possible solution for the elimination of excess cost of the PS to forestall audit disallowance as provided under Section 3.15.1 of COA Circular No 2012-003.
5.8	We further recommended the reduction of the bonuses and allowances given to each officials and employees since diminution of basic salary is not allowed.

6.7	We recommended that the Management utilize the SEF in pursuance to the purposes for which it was established as stated in Section 272 of the IRR of R.A. 7160 and Section 4, DepEd-DBM-DILG Joint Circular No. 1, s. 2017. Return to the fund the total amount of prohibited disbursements of P515,613.80.

7.6	We recommended that the Management expedite the implementation of all pending and on-going funded programs under the 20% DF including backlog PPAs from the previous years in order to optimize the utilization thereof and timely bring the desired benefits to the intended recipients.

8.6	We recommended that Management comply with the proper utilization of LDRRMF and guarantee that disbursements are in relation to disaster prevention, mitigation, preparedness, response, rehabilitation and recovery projects and activities. We further recommended that Management return the total amount of prohibited disbursements of P445,071.88 to the Special Trust Fund.

9.5	We recommended that the LDRRMO submit the Report on the Sources and Utilizations of LDRRMF, duly prepared and certified correct by the Municipal Accountant, on or before the 15th day after the end of each month to the Office of the Auditor as prescribed under COA Circular No. 2012-002 dated September 12, 2012.

10.7	We recommended that the Management take into practice the submission of complete accounts in order to support reports that are relevant to audit and submit the lacking MDRRM accounts in order to validate the transferred amount.

11.8	We recommended that Management timely submit to the Auditor the hardcopies of monthly trial balances, quarterly financial statements, monthly journals and reports as well as complete disbursement vouchers and vouchers on collections and deposits, in compliance with COA Circular No. 2009-006 and Section 111 of PD 1445. Furthermore, request for the submission of important reports and documents shall be observed as directed by the same PD.

12.6	We recommended that the Management direct concerned municipal officials and employees to strictly adhere to the provisions of R.A. No. 9184 and its Implementing Rules and Regulations and stop the practice of procuring cell cards thru reimbursement for it is not a mode of procurement. Any alternative mode of procurement that may be resorted to should be for exceptional cases that the same could justifiably apply.

13.5	We recommended that Management enforce immediate settlement of the disallowances and comply with Section 7.1.1 of the Revised Rules and Regulations on Settlement of Accounts as implemented under COA Circular No. 2009 – 006.

15.7	We recommended that Management:
Require each employee to register their attendance on the bundy clock or biometrics machine and continue maintenance of logbooks;
Assign a CCTV or a guard to closely monitor the employees who use the bundy clock or biometrics; and
Attach the certified true copies of the DTRs or the computer-generated DTRs based on biometric data to prove the accuracy and reliability of attendance for the month and to encourage truthful reporting of the number of days worked as well as number of days of absences to be properly accounted for in the computation of salaries and wages.
17.8	We recommended that the Management refund the amount of P55,955.88 since this amount was in excess of what should only be paid to the acting officials.

18.7	We recommended that the Management refund the excess payment of P53,798.19 and follow the new rate and guidelines provided by the Joint Circular in giving Subsistence, Laundry and Quarters Allowance.
19.4	We recommended that the Management refund the payment of P24,200.00 and refrain from providing such allowance.
20.6	We recommended that Management require their hired job order and contract of service workers to prepare a daily Accomplishment Report.

21.5	We recommended that the Management adhere to the above-cited regulations and for the Sangguniang Bayan members, instead of submitting a certificate or evidence of service rendered or approved DTR, the attendance in regular sessions and minutes of meetings should be submitted. When the officials and employees are on leave or on official business, the approved leave forms, travel orders and corresponding certificates of appearances should also be attached in the DTR.

22.7	We recommended that the Management follow the rate and rules prescribed by the DBM in granting RATA to officials and employees, and be consistent with the submitted documents and reports in support of the claim. We further recommended that the amount of P167,062.50 be refunded since this amount was an excess of what should only be paid to the officials and employees.

23.6	We recommended that the Disbursing Officer submit liquidation reports or issue an Official Receipt for P1,360,187.64 to settle his unliquidated cash advances. Furthermore, we recommended for the liquidation of his cash advances within its legal deadline in accordance with the applicable rules and regulations. Any unexpended balance of the cash advance should be refunded and be issued with an Official Receipt to support his liquidation.

25.6	We recommended that the Accounting Department records only transactions that are complete as to documentary requirements and are already paid, in order to avoid discrepancies between accounting and treasury records.

26.4	We recommended that all the Accountable Officers strictly adhere to Section 101 (1) and (2) of PD 1445, item (f) of Section 305 of the Local Government Code of 1991 and COA Circular 2006-005. Moreover, the fidelity bond should be compliant with the Schedule issued by the Bureau of the Treasury (provision of Treasury Order No. 1-99) in order not to be sanctioned for failure to comply with the provisions of the subject order.

27.6	We recommended that the Liquidating Officer prepare separate cash books and separate reports on collections and deposits for the two funds under her accountability in compliance with the above-cited provisions of the New Government Accounting System for Local Government Units.

28.6	We recommended that the Treasury Office sustain a reliable logbook of accountable forms and religiously prepare monthly Report of Accountability for Accountable Forms, indicating therein the procured and unissued accountable forms. Furthermore, we recommended that the unpresented accountable forms during the cash exam be presented to the Office for proper recording.

29.5	We recommended that the Disbursing Officer stop such practice of disbursing funds unless the claims are drawn on approved vouchers to avoid disallowance in audit. Require the concerned officials and employees to return the cash borrowed from the Disbursing Officer for him to refund to the municipal coffers.

33.4	We recommended that the Accounting Department regularly withhold and remit to the BIR the 3% percentage tax due from every payment made to JO and COS workers as well as to pakyaw laborers of the Municipality within the prescribed period.

34.5	We recommended that the Management strictly approve travels for trainings and seminars where such congruence between the duties and responsibilities of the attending employees and the seminar itself exists. We further recommended that the Human Resource Office intervene in recommending the approval of travels whether the sponsor of the seminar is a government agency, an NGO or a private institution.
